title: Moses Born and Hidden in a Basket
description: A Levite woman gave birth to a son and hid him for three months to protect him from Pharaoh's decree to kill all Hebrew male infants. When she could hide him no longer, she placed him in a papyrus basket coated with tar and pitch and set it among the reeds along the Nile. His sister Miriam stood watch nearby to see what would happen to him.

title: Pharaoh's Daughter Finds Moses
description: Pharaoh's daughter came to the Nile to bathe and noticed the basket among the reeds. She opened it, saw the crying Hebrew baby, and had compassion on him. Miriam quickly offered to find a Hebrew woman to nurse the child, and Pharaoh's daughter agreed — unknowingly hiring Moses' own mother. The child was eventually brought to Pharaoh's daughter, who named him Moses, meaning 'drawn out of the water.'

title: Moses Raised in Pharaoh's Palace
description: Moses grew up as a son in Pharaoh's household, educated in all the wisdom of the Egyptians and powerful in speech and action. Though raised in the heights of Egyptian royalty, Moses retained an identity with his Hebrew people. Stephen later noted that Moses was forty years old when he decided to visit his own people the Israelites.

title: Moses Kills an Egyptian and Flees to Midian
description: After witnessing an Egyptian beating a Hebrew slave, Moses killed the Egyptian and hid the body in the sand. When Pharaoh heard of the act and sought to kill Moses, Moses fled east to the land of Midian. There he sat down by a well, where he met the daughters of Reuel (Jethro) and eventually settled, marrying Zipporah and beginning a new chapter of his life as a shepherd.

title: God Reveals His Name YHWH to Moses
description: When Moses asked God what name he should give the Israelites as the name of their deliverer, God declared 'I AM WHO I AM' — the divine name YHWH. God said this was His name forever and the name by which He should be remembered from generation to generation. This revelation of the divine name marked a new epoch in Israel's understanding of God as the covenant-keeping Lord.

title: Moses and Aaron Appear Before Pharaoh
description: Moses and Aaron approached Pharaoh with God's command: 'Let my people go, so that they may hold a festival to me in the wilderness.' Pharaoh scoffed and refused, declaring he did not know YHWH and had no intention of releasing Israel. Instead, he increased the Israelites' burdens by requiring them to gather their own straw while maintaining the same brick quota, intensifying their suffering.

title: Plague 1: The Nile Turned to Blood
description: Aaron struck the Nile with his staff and all the water of Egypt — rivers, canals, ponds, and reservoirs — turned to blood. The fish died, the river stank, and the Egyptians could not drink from it. Pharaoh's heart remained hard as his magicians replicated the sign, and he returned to his palace unmoved.

title: Plague 2: Frogs Cover Egypt
description: Aaron stretched his hand over Egypt's waters and frogs swarmed up to cover every part of the land — invading houses, bedrooms, beds, ovens, and kneading troughs. Pharaoh's magicians replicated the plague but could not remove it, and Pharaoh pleaded with Moses to pray for the frogs' removal, promising to let Israel go. When the frogs died and were piled in heaps, Pharaoh hardened his heart again.

title: Plague 3: Gnats from the Dust
description: Aaron struck the dust of the ground with his staff and gnats infested all of Egypt, swarming on people and animals alike. Pharaoh's magicians attempted to replicate this plague but failed and declared to Pharaoh, 'This is the finger of God.' Yet Pharaoh's heart remained hardened, and he refused to listen.

title: Plague 4: Swarms of Flies
description: Dense swarms of flies filled the houses of Pharaoh and his officials and throughout Egypt, while Goshen where the Israelites lived was miraculously spared. Pharaoh negotiated twice, first offering to let Israel sacrifice within Egypt and then agreeing to let them go into the wilderness, but each time revoked his word once the flies were removed.

title: Plague 5: Livestock Disease
description: A devastating plague struck the livestock of Egypt — horses, donkeys, camels, cattle, sheep, and goats all died from a severe disease. God made a distinction between Israel's livestock and Egypt's: not one animal belonging to the Israelites died. Pharaoh investigated and confirmed that Israel's animals were unharmed, yet still his heart was hardened.

title: Plague 6: Festering Boils
description: Moses took handfuls of soot from a furnace and tossed it into the air before Pharaoh, and festering boils broke out on people and animals throughout Egypt. The magicians could not stand before Moses because the boils had broken out on them as well. God hardened Pharaoh's heart, and as the Lord had told Moses, Pharaoh would not listen.

title: Plague 7: Devastating Hail
description: Moses stretched out his staff toward the sky and the Lord sent the worst hailstorm Egypt had ever seen, accompanied by thunder and lightning that struck down people, animals, and every plant in the fields. Only the land of Goshen was spared. Pharaoh briefly confessed his sin and asked Moses to stop the hail, but when the storm ceased he hardened his heart once more.

title: Plague 8: Locusts Devour Egypt
description: An east wind brought a swarm of locusts so dense that nothing green remained in any tree or plant throughout Egypt — they devoured everything the hail had left. Even Pharaoh's officials begged him to release Israel, recognizing Egypt was ruined. Pharaoh summoned Moses and temporarily agreed to let the men go, but God hardened his heart and he refused to release all the people.

title: Plague 9: Thick Darkness over Egypt
description: Moses stretched his hand toward the sky and a thick, tangible darkness fell over Egypt for three days — so dense that no one could see or move. Yet all the Israelites had light in their dwellings. Pharaoh offered to let the people go but insisted their flocks and herds remain; Moses refused this compromise. Pharaoh threatened Moses' life if he appeared before him again.

title: Plague 10: Death of Egypt's Firstborn
description: God announced the final and most devastating plague: at midnight He would pass through Egypt and every firstborn — from Pharaoh's son on the throne to the firstborn of the prisoner in the dungeon — would die. A loud wailing went up throughout Egypt unlike anything before or after. This judgment fell on all Egyptian gods and on Pharaoh himself, finally breaking his resistance and compelling him to release Israel.

title: Israelites Plunder Egypt Before Leaving
description: The Egyptians, terrified by the death of their firstborn and desperate to expel Israel, urged the people to leave in haste and gave them whatever they asked. The Israelites asked their neighbors for silver, gold, and clothing, and the Egyptians gave generously. Thus Israel plundered Egypt, fulfilling God's earlier promise to Abraham that his descendants would come out with great possessions.

title: Israelites March from Rameses to Succoth
description: About six hundred thousand men on foot, along with women, children, and a mixed multitude, left Rameses and journeyed to Succoth. They carried unleavened bread dough on their shoulders because they had no time to prepare provisions. After 430 years to the day since Israel had settled in Egypt, the Lord brought all the Israelite divisions out — a night of watching kept for the Lord by all Israelites for generations.

title: Pillar of Cloud and Fire Guides Israel
description: The Lord went before Israel in a pillar of cloud by day to guide their way, and in a pillar of fire by night to give them light, so they could travel both day and night. Neither the pillar of cloud by day nor the pillar of fire by night departed from before the people. This supernatural guidance demonstrated God's constant presence and care for the nation throughout their wilderness journey.

title: Israel Trapped at the Red Sea, Pharaoh Pursues
description: When Pharaoh learned that Israel had fled, he changed his mind and pursued them with six hundred of Egypt's best chariots plus all the other chariots of Egypt. The Egyptian army caught up to the Israelites as they camped by the sea, and the people were terrified. In fear they cried out to Moses, saying it would have been better to serve the Egyptians than to die in the wilderness.

title: Song of Moses After the Red Sea Crossing
description: After the miraculous crossing and the destruction of Pharaoh's army, Moses and the Israelites sang a great song of praise to the Lord: 'I will sing to the Lord, for He is highly exalted.' Miriam the prophetess led the women in dancing with tambourines, singing the refrain. This song celebrated God's power, holiness, and covenant faithfulness as the mighty deliverer of Israel.

title: Bitter Water at Marah Made Sweet
description: After three days in the Wilderness of Shur without water, Israel arrived at Marah only to find the water too bitter to drink. The people grumbled against Moses asking what they would drink. God showed Moses a piece of wood; when Moses threw it into the water, the water became sweet. There God gave Israel a decree and a ruling, testing them and promising health if they obeyed.

title: Israelites Camp at Elim by Twelve Springs
description: After the trial at Marah, Israel arrived at Elim, a refreshing oasis in the wilderness that had twelve springs of water and seventy palm trees. The entire congregation camped there by the water. This verdant rest stop provided physical and spiritual refreshment before Israel pressed on into the harsh wilderness of Sin.

title: God Provides Manna in the Wilderness
description: When the Israelites grumbled about having no food in the Wilderness of Sin, God promised to rain bread from heaven each morning and provide quail each evening. Every morning a thin flaky substance covered the ground — the people called it 'manna,' meaning 'What is it?' It tasted like wafers made with honey and was to be gathered daily, with a double portion on the sixth day before the Sabbath.

title: Water Flows from the Rock at Rephidim
description: At Rephidim, the Israelites found no water and quarreled with Moses, saying 'Give us water to drink.' The place was called Massah ('testing') and Meribah ('quarreling') because of Israel's strife. God commanded Moses to strike the rock at Horeb with his staff, and water gushed out for all the people to drink, demonstrating God's ongoing supernatural provision even in Israel's faithlessness.

title: Joshua Defeats the Amalekites at Rephidim
description: When the Amalekites attacked Israel at Rephidim, Moses sent Joshua to fight them while he stood on the hilltop with the staff of God. As long as Moses held up his hands, Israel prevailed; when he lowered them, Amalek gained the advantage. Aaron and Hur held up Moses' arms until sunset, and Joshua defeated the Amalekite army. God declared perpetual war against Amalek and Moses built an altar called 'The Lord Is My Banner.'

title: Jethro Visits Moses and Advises Appointing Judges
description: Moses' father-in-law Jethro brought Zipporah and Moses' two sons to meet him in the wilderness. After hearing all God had done for Israel, Jethro offered burnt offerings and praised God. Observing Moses spending all day judging the people alone, Jethro gave wise counsel: appoint capable men as judges over groups of thousands, hundreds, fifties, and tens to share the load. Moses followed this advice, establishing Israel's first judicial system.

title: The Ten Commandments Given at Mount Sinai
description: God spoke the Ten Commandments directly to Israel from the top of Mount Sinai amid thunder, lightning, thick cloud, and the blast of a trumpet. The Decalogue established the moral foundation of Israel's covenant relationship with God: exclusive worship of YHWH, prohibition of idols, honoring the Sabbath, honoring parents, and laws against murder, adultery, theft, false witness, and coveting. The people trembled and stood far away, asking Moses to be their mediator.

title: The Book of the Covenant Proclaimed
description: Following the Decalogue, God gave Moses an extensive body of civil, criminal, and religious laws known as the Book of the Covenant, covering topics from treatment of servants and murder to property disputes, care for the poor, and religious festivals. Moses read these laws aloud to Israel, and the people responded, 'Everything the Lord has said we will do.' Moses then sprinkled the blood of the covenant on the people, ratifying the agreement.

title: Moses Spends Forty Days on Mount Sinai
description: The glory of the Lord settled on Mount Sinai and the cloud covered it for six days; on the seventh day God called to Moses from within the cloud. Moses entered the cloud and went up the mountain, remaining there forty days and forty nights. During this time God gave him detailed instructions for constructing the Tabernacle and all its furnishings, along with the two stone tablets inscribed with the commandments.

title: Golden Calf Made While Moses Is on the Mountain
description: When the people saw that Moses delayed coming down from the mountain, they gathered around Aaron and demanded gods to lead them. Aaron collected their golden earrings and fashioned them into a golden calf, proclaiming it the god who brought them out of Egypt. The people rose early the next day to offer sacrifices and engage in revelry around the idol, provoking God's fierce anger while Moses was still on the mountain receiving the law.

title: Moses Smashes the Stone Tablets
description: As Moses descended the mountain carrying the two stone tablets, he saw the golden calf and the dancing, and his anger burned hot. He threw the tablets down and shattered them at the foot of the mountain, symbolizing Israel's breaking of the covenant they had just made. He then burned the calf, ground it to powder, scattered it on water, and made the Israelites drink it in judgment.

title: God Renews the Covenant and Gives New Tablets
description: God commanded Moses to chisel two new stone tablets like the first and come up the mountain again. Moses spent another forty days and nights with God without eating or drinking. The Lord passed before Moses proclaiming His name and character — compassionate, gracious, slow to anger, abounding in love and faithfulness. God renewed the covenant and re-inscribed the Ten Commandments on the new tablets.

title: Moses' Face Shines After Meeting with God
description: When Moses descended from Mount Sinai after speaking with God, his face was radiant because he had spoken with the Lord — though Moses himself was unaware of it. Aaron and all the Israelites were afraid to come near him because of the radiance. Moses wore a veil over his face when speaking to the people, but removed it when he went in to speak with God. This reflected glory testified to the transformative power of God's presence.

title: Tabernacle Filled with the Glory of God
description: When Moses finished setting up the Tabernacle exactly as God commanded, the cloud covered the tent of meeting and the glory of the Lord filled the Tabernacle so powerfully that Moses could not enter it. From that day forward, whenever the cloud lifted from above the Tabernacle, the Israelites set out on their journey; when the cloud did not lift, they did not set out but waited. The pillar of cloud by day and fire by night was visible to all Israel throughout their journey.

title: Nadab and Abihu Offer Strange Fire and Die
description: Aaron's sons Nadab and Abihu took their censers, put fire in them and added incense, and offered unauthorized fire before the Lord that He had not commanded. Fire came out from the presence of the Lord and consumed them, and they died before the Lord. Moses told Aaron that this was what God had meant when He said, 'Among those who approach me I will be proved holy; in the sight of all the people I will be honored.'

title: Aaron's Rod Buds and Blossoms
description: After Korah's rebellion challenged priestly authority, God commanded each tribal leader to write his name on a staff and place all twelve staffs in the Tent of Meeting overnight. The next morning, Aaron's staff representing the tribe of Levi had budded, blossomed, and produced almonds. God instructed Moses to place Aaron's staff before the ark of the covenant as a permanent sign to silence the rebellious and prevent further grumbling.

title: Korah's Rebellion Swallowed by the Earth
description: Korah, Dathan, Abiram, and 250 community leaders rose up against Moses and Aaron, claiming the entire congregation was holy and accusing them of lifting themselves above the assembly. God instructed Moses to have the rebels stand at the entrance of their tents; then the ground under Korah, Dathan, and Abiram split open and swallowed them alive with their households. Fire also came out from the Lord and consumed the 250 men who were offering incense.

title: Miriam Dies at Kadesh
description: In the first month of the fortieth year in the wilderness, the entire Israelite community arrived at the Desert of Zin and stopped at Kadesh. There Miriam, the prophetess and sister of Moses and Aaron who had led Israel's women in worship at the Red Sea, died and was buried. The community had no water, and a new crisis quickly followed her death.

title: Moses Strikes the Rock at Meribah
description: When the people quarreled about the lack of water at Kadesh, God told Moses to speak to the rock before the assembly and it would pour out water. Instead, Moses struck the rock twice with his staff in anger, saying 'Must we bring you water out of this rock?' Water gushed out abundantly, but God told Moses and Aaron that because they did not trust Him enough to honor His holiness, neither of them would lead Israel into the Promised Land.

title: Aaron Dies on Mount Hor
description: The Lord told Moses that Aaron would die at Mount Hor because of his disobedience at Meribah — he would not enter the Promised Land. Moses, Aaron, and Aaron's son Eleazar climbed Mount Hor in sight of the whole community. There Moses removed Aaron's priestly garments and put them on Eleazar, and Aaron died on the top of the mountain. When the community learned Aaron had died, they mourned for thirty days.

title: The Bronze Serpent Heals Israel in the Wilderness
description: The Israelites became impatient on the journey around Edom and spoke against God and Moses, and the Lord sent venomous snakes among the people causing many to die. When the people repented, God instructed Moses to make a bronze snake and put it on a pole; anyone who was bitten could look at it and live. Jesus later cited this episode as a foreshadowing of His own lifting up on the cross for the salvation of all who believe.

title: Israel Defeats Sihon King of the Amorites
description: When Israel sent messengers asking Sihon king of the Amorites for peaceful passage through his territory, Sihon refused and came out to fight against Israel in the desert. Israel struck him down and took possession of his land from the Arnon to the Jabbok, including the Amorite capital of Heshbon. This was Israel's first major military victory in the Transjordan region, opening the way for settlement east of the Jordan River.

title: Israel Defeats Og King of Bashan
description: After defeating Sihon, Israel turned north and Og king of Bashan came out with his entire army to meet them at Edrei. The Lord told Moses not to be afraid, for He had given Og into Israel's hands as He had done with Sihon. Israel killed Og, his sons, and his entire army, leaving no survivors, and took possession of the land of Bashan. Og was noted for his extraordinary size, his iron bed being nine cubits long.

title: Balaam's Oracles Bless Israel Instead of Cursing
description: Balak king of Moab hired the prophet Balaam to curse Israel, but God repeatedly intervened and Balaam could only bless Israel four times instead of cursing them. In his oracles, Balaam prophesied of a star that would come out of Jacob and a scepter from Israel — widely understood as a messianic prediction. Balak's plan entirely failed as every attempt to curse Israel turned into blessing.

title: Phinehas Stops the Plague with a Spear
description: While Israel was weeping at the entrance of the tent of meeting over their sin of worshiping Baal of Peor, an Israelite man brazenly brought a Midianite woman into the camp. Phinehas, grandson of Aaron, took a spear and drove it through both of them, stopping a plague that had already killed twenty-four thousand. God granted Phinehas a covenant of a lasting priesthood because of his zeal for God's honor.

title: Second Census of Israel Taken in Moab
description: After the plague, God commanded Moses and Eleazar to take a census of all the Israelite men twenty years old or more who could serve in the army. The total came to 601,730 men — slightly fewer than the 603,550 counted at Sinai forty years earlier. Of the original men counted at Sinai, only Caleb and Joshua remained, as God had said; all others had perished in the wilderness.

title: Cities of Refuge Established in Canaan and Transjordan
description: God commanded Israel to designate six cities of refuge — three on each side of the Jordan — where a person who had accidentally killed someone could flee and receive protection from the avenger of blood until standing trial before the community. These cities were to be accessible to both Israelites and foreigners, establishing a principle of due process and asylum within the covenant community.

title: Moses' Farewell Address on the Plains of Moab
description: In the fortieth year, on the first day of the eleventh month, Moses began expounding the law to all Israel on the plains of Moab opposite Suph. The book of Deuteronomy records Moses' three great farewell speeches — a historical review, a re-proclamation of the law, and a covenant renewal — as Israel stood on the verge of entering the Promised Land without him. Moses urged Israel to remember God's faithfulness and obey His commands in the land they were about to enter.

title: Moses Blesses the Twelve Tribes
description: Before his death, Moses pronounced blessings over each of the twelve tribes of Israel in a poem echoing Jacob's earlier blessings in Genesis. Each blessing spoke prophetically to the tribe's character, calling, and future role in the land. Moses concluded with a declaration of the incomparability of God as Israel's shield and helper, and of Israel's unique privilege as a people saved by the Lord.

title: Moses Views the Promised Land from Mount Nebo
description: God led Moses up from the plains of Moab to the top of Pisgah, opposite Jericho, and showed him the entire Promised Land — from Gilead to Dan, Naphtali, Ephraim and Manasseh, all of Judah to the Mediterranean Sea, the Negev, and the Jordan Valley as far as Zoar. God told Moses, 'This is the land I promised on oath to Abraham, Isaac and Jacob. I have let you see it with your eyes, but you will not cross over into it.'

title: Moses Dies and Is Buried by God
description: Moses, servant of the Lord, died in Moab at the age of 120, his eyes still undimmed and his strength unabated. God Himself buried Moses in a valley in Moab opposite Beth Peor, but to this day no one knows where his grave is. The Israelites grieved for Moses on the plains of Moab for thirty days. No prophet since has risen in Israel like Moses, whom the Lord knew face to face.

title: Joshua Appointed as Moses' Successor
description: Joshua son of Nun was filled with the spirit of wisdom because Moses had laid his hands on him, and the Israelites listened to him and did what the Lord had commanded Moses. God also appeared to Joshua and commissioned him directly, saying 'Be strong and courageous. Do not be afraid; do not be discouraged, for the Lord your God will be with you wherever you go.' Joshua would lead Israel across the Jordan and into the conquest of Canaan.
